title: Magnificencia Ecclesie
summary: The repository XML is copied from University of Michigan CME item CME00067 and encodes the full textual matter of Henry Noble MacCracken's twelve-page 1909 PMLA article, including his introduction, a seventeen-line Longfellow quotation, the complete 312-line Middle English poem, notes, explicit, and signature. MacCracken transcribed the poem from the sole witness listed in the consulted register, Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.21, ff. 285r-287r. The complete 686-canvas Trinity IIIF provider object is cached at 1600-pixel width, with the poem mapped only to canvases 573-577; f. 287v begins adjacent works. A complete 952-page Harvard/Google scan of the 1909 PMLA volume and complete PDF, OCR, structured OCR, and scan-data derivatives are also cached, as is the complete 1920 Brown register volume containing entry 459.
review_notes: Whole-object caching is used throughout: the Trinity bundle contains all 686 canvases exposed by its manifest, the PMLA PDF contains all 952 provider pages including bindings, issue wrappers, advertisements, and blanks, and the Brown PDF contains the complete 498-page provider object.

The work portion is a mapping inside each complete object, never a cropped substitute: the manuscript poem is ff. 285r-287r, Trinity canvases 573-577, while the MacCracken article is printed pp. 687-698 and PDF pp. 743-754 in the complete PMLA scan.

Complete means all provider-exposed canvases or pages. Trinity exposes covers, boards, leaves, flyleaves, and envelope matter but no distinct spine, head, tail, or fore-edge canvases, so its IIIF object is not a complete physical-surface study.
The medieval poem, MacCracken's editorial article, the University of Michigan encoding, provider image derivatives, and access or publication policies are modeled as distinct rights layers.

MacCracken's Westminster localization and rejection of Lydgate are edition-level arguments; the manifest does not conflate manuscript production, later codex assembly, ownership, and medieval work composition.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00067
entities.description: The XML artifact used by this repository's publication workflow through the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME submodule.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME item CME00067
entities.description: A public SGML/XML-derived encoding of MacCracken's complete PMLA article text. It distinguishes the modern introduction from the medieval poem while retaining the article's Longfellow quotation, source headnotes, footnotes, explicit, and author signature.

entities.label: Henry Noble MacCracken's Magnificencia Ecclesie
entities.description: The immediate source edition and article for CME00067. MacCracken introduces and transcribes the anonymous poem from Trinity R.3.21, rejects Lydgate's authorship, and supplies a local note about a disordered stanza and a conjecturally missing rhyme word.

bibliographic.citation: Henry Noble MacCracken, 'Magnificencia Ecclesie,' PMLA 24, no. 4 (December 1909): 687-698, https://doi.org/10.2307/456803.
bibliographic.title: Magnificencia Ecclesie

entities.label: Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.21
entities.description: A fifteenth-century composite paper manuscript of miscellaneous Latin and Middle English poems. Magnificencia Ecclesie begins at f. 285r and ends at f. 287r; the next cataloged works begin on f. 287v. The inherited contents heading attributes the poem to Lydgate, but that attribution is disputed and the work is cataloged as anonymous.

date_statements.label: manuscript production
date_statements.notes: The official whole-codex date is broader than work-specific modern dating and does not by itself date composition.

holding.notes: Given by George Wilmer; formerly owned by John Stow. The official catalog separately records the poem as item 60 at f. 285.

physical_description.format: Composite volume of miscellaneous poems in Latin and Middle English
notes: The IIIF provider sequence exposes 686 canvases from the front cover through envelope verso.
The provider manifest does not expose distinct spine, head, tail, or fore-edge photographs.

entities.label: Complete Trinity R.3.21 IIIF provider object
entities.description: The full 686-canvas color sequence exposed by Trinity's IIIF Presentation 2 manifest, from front cover through envelope verso. The poem is mapped to canvases 573-577; f. 287v on canvas 578 is adjacent content, not part of the work.
entities.reproduction_format: 686 IIIF color canvases; cached as the exact manifest, inventory, and 1600-pixel JPEG bundle

entities.label: Brown, Register of Middle English Religious and Didactic Verse, Part II
entities.description: Carleton Brown's complete 1920 register volume. Entry 459 gives the incipit and rubric for Magnificencia Ecclesie, counts thirty-nine eight-line stanzas, lists Trinity Cambridge 601 at f. 285a as the witness, and cites MacCracken's PMLA edition.

The exact IIIF object licenses attributed noncommercial sharing and adaptation under CC BY-NC 4.0. Commercial reuse, print-on-demand distribution, or uses outside that license require separate authorization; Trinity's broader publication wording is recorded as a distinct policy ambiguity.

rights.component: underlying anonymous fifteenth-century Middle English poem
rights.basis: The anonymous medieval work predates modern copyright terms by centuries; this conclusion concerns the underlying poem, not modern editions, encodings, photographs, or provider terms.

rights.component: MacCracken's 1909 article, introduction, notes, and edition text as published
rights.basis: The article was published in the United States in 1909, and the maximum federal term for that publication has expired.
Copyright duration and publication history vary by jurisdiction, and Cambridge Core currently displays a 1909 MLA copyright notice. No worldwide public-domain conclusion is made.

rights.component: University of Michigan Middle English Corpus digital encoding
rights.basis: The collection describes its manually keyed corpus editions as public-domain source texts and labels the collection No Copyright - United States; this provider statement is distinct from rights in scans or later transformations.

rights.component: Internet Archive provider object and Harvard/Google page-image derivative
rights.basis: Internet Archive applies the Public Domain Mark 1.0 to this provider object. The mark records the provider's no-known-copyright assertion and is not a license or a substitute for jurisdiction-specific analysis.

rights.component: Trinity IIIF images and provider manifest
rights.basis: The exact IIIF manifest and item page state CC BY-NC 4.0 with attribution to Trinity College Cambridge, affirmatively licensing attributed noncommercial sharing and adaptation. Trinity's broader Wren policy separately permits downloads for personal study and asks researchers to apply for publication permission.
notes: The license and broader publication-permission wording are recorded separately rather than harmonized without an item-specific institutional interpretation.
Attributed noncommercial reuse within CC BY-NC 4.0 is licensed; commercial reuse, print-on-demand distribution, and uses outside the license require separate permission.
Caching the bundle does not grant rights beyond the exact license and applicable provider terms.

rights.basis: The volume was published in 1920, and the maximum federal term for that publication has expired.

editorial_practices.representation: two top-level divisions for the introduction and poem, with page and manuscript-folio milestones
editorial_practices.description: The encoding represents the entire article's textual matter rather than extracting only the medieval poem. It keeps MacCracken's introduction, Longfellow quotation, source headnotes, poem, footnotes, explicit, and signature, and marks the structural boundary between modern paratext and medieval work.

editorial_practices.source_feature: single identified manuscript witness with a locally disordered stanza
editorial_practices.representation: diplomatic-leaning edited transcription with manuscript-folio milestones and a reconstruction note
editorial_practices.description: MacCracken names Trinity R.3.21 as his source. At one stanza he reports the scribe's marginal 'a,' proposes the order 1, 2, 8, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, and suggests that line 2 originally ended in a word such as 'vertuous.' The print and encoding retain the manuscript order while attaching the same note at two callout locations; the repeated encoded note text is not evidence of two distinct notes.
editorial_practices.scope: Middle English poem on printed pp. 688-698

editorial_practices.source_feature: manuscript abbreviations in the Latin rubric and Middle English text
editorial_practices.representation: expanded letters are distinguished typographically in italics within otherwise roman words
editorial_practices.description: Comparison of the printed article, encoding, and target manuscript pages shows MacCracken using italic letter sequences within words to signal supplied expansions. Italics also mark Latin passages and titles, so individual italic spans must be interpreted in context rather than treated mechanically as expansions.
editorial_practices.scope: rubric, Latin material, and poem on printed pp. 688-698

editorial_practices.source_feature: MacCracken's typographic distinction between manuscript letters and editorial expansions
editorial_practices.representation: HI elements with REND italic preserve print typography; capitalization inside mixed roman/italic sequences is retained
editorial_practices.description: The encoding preserves MacCracken's expansion typography through italic HI elements rather than asserting a normalized reading layer. Because the same markup also represents titles, Latin quotations, and emphasis, context remains necessary.

evidence.citation: Henry Noble MacCracken, 'Magnificencia Ecclesie,' PMLA 24, no. 4 (December 1909): 687-698.
evidence.summary: Establishes article bibliography, the complete immediate source, MacCracken's direct use of Trinity R.3.21, late-fifteenth-century translation claim, Lydgate attribution history and rejection, rubric, complete poem, local reconstruction note, explicit, and signature.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive item PMLA1909, Harvard/Google scan of PMLA volume 24 (1909).
evidence.locator: 952-page provider object; MacCracken article at PDF pp. 743-754
evidence.summary: Identifies the Harvard source copy, Google scanning, ARLIMA upload, exact derivatives, and Public Domain Mark 1.0 provider assertion.

evidence.citation: Direct visual and structural inspection of the cached complete PMLA volume 24 facsimile and derivatives.
evidence.locator: first and last bound views; volume title; December issue wrapper; PDF pp. 743-754; printed pp. 687-698; OCR OBJECT crosswalk
evidence.summary: Confirms that the 952-page provider sequence retains the complete bound volume, that the article begins on PDF p. 743 and ends on PDF p. 754, and that all matter represented by the encoding is present.
Confirms the article title, Henry Noble MacCracken, volume, issue, year, pages 687-698, DOI, research-article type, access mode, and current provider copyright notice.

evidence.citation: Trinity College Cambridge, James Catalogue of Western Manuscripts, R.3.21.
evidence.locator: main details; contents items 60-61; provenance; bibliography; item-page footer
evidence.summary: Confirms shelfmark, James number 601, fifteenth-century date, paper support, 320-folio extent, dimensions, Latin and Middle English, provenance, the poem at f. 285 with incipit and explicit, adjacent content from f. 287v, IIIF manifest, and CC BY-NC 4.0 item-page statement.

evidence.citation: Trinity College Cambridge, IIIF Presentation manifest for MS R.3.21.
evidence.locator: 686 canvases; canvases 573-577 for ff. 285r-287r; canvas 578 for adjacent f. 287v
evidence.summary: Provides the complete published canvas inventory, labels, exact Image API services, institutional attribution, and CC BY-NC 4.0 license.

evidence.citation: Integrity, inventory, and representative visual audit of the cached complete Trinity R.3.21 IIIF bundle.
evidence.locator: front cover; ff. 285r, 285v, 286r, 286v, and 287r; adjacent f. 287v; final envelope verso; ZIP manifest, inventory, and integrity test
evidence.summary: Confirms 686 of 686 images, the work's exact five-canvas start and end, manuscript rubric and incipit, explicit, adjacent content boundary, effective 1600, and full non-upscaling image profiles, complete provider inventory, and absence of distinct spine or edge canvases.

evidence.citation: Trinity College Cambridge, Wren Digital Collections: Digital Photography, Image Supply and Licensing.
evidence.summary: States that already digitized images may be downloaded for personal study, offers quoted image supply, and asks researchers to apply for permission to publish collection images.

evidence.citation: Carleton Brown, A Register of Middle English Religious & Didactic Verse, Part II (Oxford: Bibliographical Society at the University Press, 1920), entry 459.
evidence.summary: Confirms the incipit, short Latin rubric, thirty-nine eight-line stanzas, Trinity Cambridge 601 at f. 285a as the listed witness, and MacCracken's PMLA edition.

evidence.citation: Direct visual inspection of the cached complete Brown Register Part II facsimile.
evidence.locator: first and last bound views; title page; PDF p. 108 / printed p. 78 / entry 459; final printed matter
evidence.summary: Confirms the 498-page provider object is the complete Part II volume and establishes the exact register-entry crosswalk.

evidence.citation: U.S. Copyright Office, Circular 15A, Duration of Copyright.
evidence.summary: States federal term rules for works published before 1978, including the maximum ninety-five-year term used for the 1909 article and 1920 register analysis.

evidence.citation: Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988, section 12, duration of copyright in literary, dramatic, musical or artistic works.
evidence.summary: Provides the United Kingdom's duration rules for known and unknown authors, supporting only the underlying medieval-work analysis rather than a conclusion about modern editions or photographs.

open_questions.question: Can Trinity provide separate spine and head, tail, and fore-edge views absent from the otherwise complete 686-canvas R.3.21 IIIF provider object, and how does its publication-permission policy interact with the exact CC BY-NC 4.0 license?
next_steps: Ask the Wren Library for an item-specific image inventory, interpretation, and quote only if physical binding documentation or publication is needed.
Treat the cached bundle as complete only relative to the provider-published canvas inventory.
Do not infer redistribution permission from local caching.

open_questions.question: Did MacCracken state fuller principles for expansion, punctuation, capitalization, lineation, or emendation than the local evidence in his 1909 article?
next_steps: Compare every edited line against ff. 285r-287r, including superscript, deletion, punctuation, and line-boundary evidence.
Consult MacCracken's related Lydgate publications or archival papers for an explicit transcription statement.
Retain the printed typography in the encoding without treating every italic span as the same intervention category.

open_questions.question: What are the exact booklet, scribal, paper-stock, binding, and provenance histories of the R.3.21 unit containing ff. 285r-287r, and which of those layers can legitimately inform the poem's date or place?
next_steps: Consult current codicological and scribal studies cited by Trinity and inspect quire boundaries around ff. 285-287.
Distinguish the production of the target booklet from assembly or rebinding of the present composite manuscript and from the work's composition.
Do not promote MacCracken's Westminster conjecture or London-centered ownership into a composition-place assertion.

open_questions.question: What is the current copyright status of MacCracken's editorial article outside the United States, and what reuse terms does the MLA or Cambridge Core assert for its hosted edition?
next_steps: Perform country-specific term analysis before reuse outside the United States.
Ask the MLA or Cambridge Core for rights clarification if reusing article-level modern contributions in a jurisdiction where copyright may persist.
Keep the public-domain medieval poem separate from MacCracken's introduction and editorial apparatus.
